concerns.text: The plan proposes a 'strict time-series hold-out validation (2010-2020 train, 2021-2023 test)' to identify predictive thresholds. However, the primary analysis (Spearman correlation, linear regression) is performed on the *aligned events* dataset without explicitly stating that the correlation coefficients themselves are only computed on the training set or that the 'validation' is merely a threshold sweep. If the correlation is computed on the full dataset (2010-2023) and then 'validated' by a threshold sweep on 2021-2023, the correlation metric is not independently validated against the hold-out set; it is a global statistic. The plan must clarify if the core associational claim (correlation strength) is derived from the full sample (which is valid for description) or if the 'validation' step is meant to test the *predictive* power of the correlation on unseen data. If the latter, the plan lacks a mechanism to compute a correlation/prediction error on the test set, as the test set is only used for threshold sensitivity (FR-008), not for re-evaluating the correlation coefficient itself.

The plan states: 'If N < 30, a warning will be logged, and definitive threshold claims will be deferred.' This is a methodological safeguard, but the plan does not address the statistical validity of the *correlation* itself if N is small. Spearman correlation with N < 30 has very low power and wide confidence intervals. The plan treats the correlation coefficient as a fixed output regardless of N, only deferring 'threshold claims'. This creates a risk of reporting a spurious or highly unstable correlation coefficient as a 'result' when the sample size is insufficient to support even the associational claim. The analysis should either report confidence intervals for the correlation (which will be wide for small N) or explicitly state that the correlation result is exploratory and not statistically robust if N < 30.

The plan proposes using 'Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR)' for multiple comparison correction on '2 primary hypotheses' (flare→Dst, CME→Dst). While FDR is generally more powerful than Bonferroni, applying it to only two tests is statistically unusual and may be overkill or misapplied. More critically, the plan does not specify the *family* of tests being corrected. If the family includes the threshold sensitivity sweep (3 cutoffs) and the piecewise model test (FR-014), the number of tests is >2. If the family is only the two primary correlations, FDR is acceptable but Bonferroni is standard for such a small family. The plan must define the 'family' of hypotheses to ensure the correction is scientifically valid. Without this, the 'corrected p-value' may be meaningless or incorrectly calculated.

The plan states: 'If VIF > 5, the system will switch to separate univariate models or Ridge regression.' This is a valid fallback for collinearity. However, the plan does not address the *interpretation* of the results if the fallback is triggered. If the joint model is discarded due to collinearity, the 'variance explained' (R²) from the joint model is no longer a valid metric for the combined effect of flare and CME. The plan must specify how the 'variance explained' is reported in the fallback case (e.g., sum of univariate R²? max of univariate R²? or just the Ridge R²?). Reporting a joint R² when the joint model was discarded would be a scientific error.

The plan mentions 'post-hoc power analysis' using a pre-specified effect size of r=0.30. Post-hoc power analysis is widely criticized in statistics because it is a deterministic function of the observed p-value and sample size; it adds no new information beyond the p-value itself. If the observed correlation is significant, the post-hoc power will be high; if not, it will be low. This is a tautological exercise. The plan should instead focus on *a priori* power analysis (if possible) or simply report the confidence intervals for the observed effect size. Relying on post-hoc power to 'defer definitive threshold claims' is methodologically unsound and may lead to incorrect conclusions about the study's validity.
